testCase.description: B1: ingredient noise focus — chicken+broccoli dinner for 2

vars.input: I have chicken and broccoli. Please help me prepare dinner for two people.

assert.value:
Evaluate this meal suggestion for {{party_size}} people with ingredients {{input}}.
Score 1-5 on three criteria, then give an overall score (the minimum of the three):
(1) Practicality: are the dishes executable with the stated ingredients without requiring unusual tools or techniques?
(2) Count fit: is the total number of recipes between {{expected_dish_min}} and {{expected_dish_max}}?
(3) Variety: are the recipes meaningfully different (different protein treatment, cuisine, or technique — not two pan-fried chicken dishes)?
Return the overall score.

The user provided these ingredients: {{input}}. Common pantry staples (salt, pepper, oil, garlic, onion, basic spices, soy sauce, vinegar) are acceptable.
Score 1-5:
5 = All ingredients in recipes are either user-provided, pantry staples, or minor acceptable extras (1-2 per recipe).
3 = Several unlisted non-staple ingredients appear but don't dominate.
1 = Recipes are loaded with ingredients the user didn't mention (e.g., needing butter, cream, specialty produce, fresh herbs not named).
Return the score.

vars.input: I have chicken and broccoli. Please help me prepare dinner for two people.

results.error: The best-matched recipe (Gochujang Steak Bowl) uses the user's beef, egg, and rice but still requires gochujang, sesame oil, cucumber, green onions, and sesame seeds — several non-staple unlisted items. The other two recipes are worse: the Pita ignores rice and egg entirely and needs 6 specialty items, while the Za'atar bowl requires chicken (not provided) plus many specialty ingredients. Overall, the recipes contain several to many unlisted non-staple ingredients, though one recipe is a reasonable partial match.

testCase.description: B2: ingredient noise focus — beef+egg+rice dinner for 4

vars.input: I have beef, egg, and rice. Please help me to prepare dinner for four people.

assert.value:
Evaluate this meal suggestion for {{party_size}} people with ingredients {{input}}.
Score 1-5 on three criteria, then give an overall score (the minimum of the three):
(1) Practicality: are the dishes executable with the stated ingredients without requiring unusual tools or techniques?
(2) Count fit: is the total number of recipes between {{expected_dish_min}} and {{expected_dish_max}}?
(3) Variety: are the recipes meaningfully different (different protein treatment, cuisine, or technique — not two pan-fried chicken dishes)?
Return the overall score.

The user provided these ingredients: {{input}}. Common pantry staples (salt, pepper, oil, garlic, onion, basic spices, soy sauce, vinegar) are acceptable.
Score 1-5:
5 = All ingredients in recipes are either user-provided, pantry staples, or minor acceptable extras (1-2 per recipe).
3 = Several unlisted non-staple ingredients appear but don't dominate.
1 = Recipes are loaded with ingredients the user didn't mention (e.g., needing butter, cream, specialty produce, fresh herbs not named).
Return the score.

vars.input: I have beef, egg, and rice. Please help me to prepare dinner for four people.

results.error: Practicality is strong (all dishes are executable with standard vegetarian/dairy-free ingredients and common tools). Count fits the 2–3 range with exactly 3 recipes. However, variety is poor: two of the three recipes (Mapo Tofu and Tofu & Bok Choy Stir-Fry) are both Chinese stir-fries with nearly identical structure (tofu protein, stir-fry technique, soy/sesame sauce, rice carb), making them not meaningfully different. The overall score is the minimum of the three criteria scores: min(5,5,2) = 2.

Prep the noodles and sauce: Soak noodles to rehydrate per package directions. Mix all sauce ingredients in a container until well combined. 2. Stir fry the eggs and vegetables: Heat wok or large pan over high heat with oil. Add beaten egg, swirl around pan until fluffy with browning, chop and set aside. Add more oil, garlic, and white pepper; bloom for 30-45 seconds. Add chopped broccolini, stir fry 1-2 minutes, add a drizzle of sauce, then remove to bowl with eggs. 3. Brown the chicken: Add oil to wok, add chicken slices, brown 2 minutes per side, add sauce for color and seasoning. 4. Bring it all together: Add broccolini and egg back to wok with drained noodles. Add enough sauce to coat, stir fry 5-7 minutes until noodles soften and absorb sauce. Sear protein until browned and cooked through. Remove and set aside. Add oil, crack in eggs, scramble until cooked, set aside with protein. Wipe wok.\n3. Drain noodles. Add oil to wok, add dried shrimp and bloom 30 seconds until fragrant. Add garlic and ginger, stir fry 15-30 seconds. Add noodles, reserved egg and protein, and sauce. Toss until coated.\n4. Add scallions and bamboo shoots in final minute. Taste and adjust with fish sauce, sugar, or lime. Serve with peanuts, lime wedges, and green onions.","alternatives":[]},{"id":"r074","name":"Thai Chile Pork & Cabbage Stir Fry","name_zh":"泰式辣椒猪肉炒卷心菜","cuisine":"Thai","cooking_method":"stir-fry","effort_level":"medium","flavor_tags":["aromatic","spicy","umami"],"serves":2,"pcsv_roles":{"protein":["ground pork","eggs"],"veggie":["cabbage","thai chilies","shallots"],"sauce":["salt","cooking oil","fish sauce","msg","sesame oil","black sesame seeds"]},"ingredients_have":[],"ingredients_need":["ground pork","cabbage","salt","thai chilies","shallots","cooking oil","fish sauce","msg","sesame oil","eggs","black sesame seeds"],"ingredients":[{"name":"ground pork","amount":"225 g","pcsv":["protein"]},{"name":"cabbage","amount":"1/4, thinly sliced","pcsv":["veggie"]},{"name":"salt","amount":"a sprinkle","pcsv":["sauce"]},{"name":"thai chilies","amount":"3-8, thinly sliced","pcsv":["veggie"]},{"name":"shallots","amount":"1, thinly sliced","pcsv":["veggie"]},{"name":"cooking oil","amount":"as needed","pcsv":["sauce"]},{"name":"fish sauce","amount":"a spoonful","pcsv":["sauce"]},{"name":"msg","amount":"a sprinkle","pcsv":["sauce"]},{"name":"sesame oil","amount":"a drizzle","pcsv":["sauce"]},{"name":"eggs","amount":"1, fried","pcsv":["protein"]},{"name":"black sesame seeds","amount":"a sprinkle","pcsv":["sauce"]}],"instructions":"1. Brown the pork: Preheat a pan over medium-high heat. Add the ground pork and press it into the hot pan, making a thin even layer across the hot surface. Allow the pork to sear undisturbed until the bottom begins to brown and take on some color. Using a potato masher, fork, or spatula, break up the pork into small pebble-sized bits. 2. Add the cabbage and steam: Once the pork is mostly cooked through, add in the sliced cabbage. Stir the cabbage into the meat to ensure nothing is burning on the bottom of the pan. Cover to steam and soften the cabbage for a minute or two. Remove the cover. Sauté for another minute or two to get more browning on the meat and edges of the cabbage and evaporate any excess moisture. 3. Finish with aromatics and sauces: Push the cabbage and pork mixture to one side of the pan. Add a drizzle of oil to the open space and add in the shallot and Thai chilies to bloom for about 30 seconds before mixing everything together. Add the fish sauce to deglaze any browning from the bottom of the pan. Turn off the heat. Add a drizzle of sesame oil and a sprinkle of MSG. Taste and adjust as needed. 4. Plate and serve: Serve with a fried egg and a sprinkle of sesame seeds for garnish.","alternatives":[]}]}}},{"event_type":"explanation","data":{"event_type":"explanation","text":"You’ve got a solid base, but the PCSV balance is sauce-light, so I’d lean toward a halal-friendly chicken rice bowl or stir-fry that adds a quick savory sauce.\n\nBest match:\n- Chicken and Ginger Stir Fry / 姜丝炒鸡 — Asian, stir-fry, medium, serves 2, from the recipe KB\n  - You already have: rice, broccoli\n  - You’d need: chicken thigh, ginger, garlic, soy sauce, honey, vegetable oil\n  - Note: it uses chicken thigh in the KB, but chicken breast can usually work as a leaner swap if you cook it briefly\n\nOther KB option:\n- Za'atar Chicken & Rice Bowl with Pomegranate Seeds / 扎塔尔鸡肉石榴籽米饭碗 — Egyptian, medium, serves 2, from the recipe KB\n  - You already have: chicken breast, rice\n  - You’d need several spices and toppings, so it’s a bit more pantry-dependent\n\nBecause you need halal recipes, I’d avoid the non-halal alternatives that include bacon, guanciale, or fish/oyster sauce if that matters for your kitchen. Rest for a few minutes before dicing.\n\nStep 3: Assemble the bowl. Return the score.
